title: Melbourne woman's desperate plea to save disabled sister from deportation
text:
The sister of a woman with cerebral palsy who is facing deportation has issued a desperate plea for the Australian government to let her stay.

Jacqueline Schmidt Aravena, 61, has been living with her sister Marcela in Melbourne for nine years, after originally coming to Australia from her native Chile on a visitor visa in 2017.

Marcela, an aged care and disability support worker, said she had been looking after Jacqueline, who also has an intellectual disability, for most of her life - fulfilling a promise she made to her mother on her deathbed more than 40 years ago.

Jacqueline Schmidt Aravena, pictured with her sister at their Melbourne home. (Supplied: Marcela Schmidt Aravena)

"My mother had cancer and the night before she passed away, she said, 'What is going to happen with Jacqueline?" Marcela said.

"I said, 'Don't worry, Jacqueline is going to be okay. I will take care of her.'"

Marcela acted as Jacqueline's main caregiver for decades until 2005, when she fell in love and moved to Melbourne to be with her Australian partner. She is now an Australian citizen.

Having been advised it would be impossible to get a visa for her sister to come with her, Marcela paid for a carer to look after Jacqueline while they were apart, and flew home as much as she could.

When Jacqueline's carer told Marcela she could no longer do the job, she arranged with the help of a member of parliament for her sister to come to Australia and apply for a remaining relative visa.

Marcela said her sister was happy and content living with her and her family and did not rely on the government for any support.

"My children grew up with Jacqueline and now our grandkids are growing up around her as well," she said.

"They love her, she is like a little girl, mentally, so she likes playing with them.

"Normally, she does everything with me. I take her shopping, and everything I do, she's in the car with me."

Jacqueline celebrated her 60th birthday last year with her family in Australia. (Supplied: Marcela Schmidt Aravena)

In 2019, Jacqueline's visa application was refused, under the grounds that she had other relatives in Chile. For the remaining visa to be approved, an applicant's living relatives must all reside in Australia.

However, Marcela said her sister's half-siblings in Chile were elderly, aged in their 80s and 90s, and were unable to care for her. The pair's brother had also been estranged from the family for 20 years.

In 2022, the Administrative Appeals Tribunal upheld the department's decision, despite noting the limited capacity of Jaqueline's other relatives to care for her.

Two requests for a ministerial intervention into Jacqueline's case have also been knocked back, with the second being refused in December 2025.

The family's lawyer, Michael Cao from Challenge Legal, said both times the application never made it to the minister's desk for consideration, because new personal procedural directions had narrowed the scope for intervention in cases like hers under compassionate grounds.

"The minister's office found she doesn't meet the new, narrowed criteria," Cao said. "They didn't even refer the case to the minister, which we believe is quite unfair and unfortunate for Jacqueline and her family."

"We like to believe that Jacqueline did always satisfy the compassionate grounds because she has no family anywhere else that can care for her," Cao said.

READ MORE: Teen dies after being hit by two cars in Perth after getting off bus to go to school ﻿

Marcela said her sister was well loved and cared for here in Australia, but "would not survive" in Chile. (Supplied: Marcela Schmidt Aravena)

Cao has since lodged a third application for a ministerial intervention, with the written support of Greens senator David Shoebridge.

Marcela said if her sister was deported back to Chile, it would be devastating for the whole family.

"She is not going to survive," Marcela said.

"She would have to go to a nursing home, and nursing homes in Chile are nothing like they are here."

The uncertainty has left the family in a harrowing state of limbo. Jacqueline's bridging visa expired on Thursday, and the family spent the day waiting for news on a renewal while their third application for intervention sits with the department.

READ MORE: Man allegedly planned terrorist attack on multiple locations in Perth ﻿

Marcela has now turned to the public for help, launching a Change.org petition which has so far attracted more than 1800 signatures.

"We are not asking for special treatment or any kind of financial assistance. We are asking for humanity," Marcela wrote in the petition.

title: Sienna survived childhood cancer. Then, her heart began to fail
text:
Chemotherapy helped save Sienna Howkins's life as a four-year-old, but it damaged her heart so much, she needed a heart transplant as a teenager.

The 20-year-old, who received a donor heart a year ago, is studying to be a nurse, inspired by those who have taken care of her.

She's one of about 1,400 Australians who had a life-saving organ transplant last year after more than 500 people donated organs after death nationally, according to new DonateLife data.

Sienna Howkins has been able to restart dancing and play netball for the first time since receiving a heart translant. (Supplied)

The Queensland woman from Logan, south of Brisbane, was given just a 30 per cent chance of survival when she was diagnosed with rhabdomyosarcoma, a type of cancer, in early childhood.

"It was about the size of a football when they found it," Sienna said.

"It was the day after my best friend's birthday. I stayed at her house the night. No symptom whatsoever."

But then she was taken to a doctor after jumping on a trampoline and falling over.

"All of a sudden I complained of my stomach hurting and they just felt the cancer straight away because of how large it was," Sienna recalled.

Sienna Howkins, pictured with her siblings Ben and Chelsea, survived childhood cancer. (Supplied)

Rhabdomyosarcoma is a rare and aggressive soft tissue cancer.

In Sienna's case, the tumour had developed in her left kidney and spread to her lungs.

She had chemotherapy, surgery to remove the tumour, and radiation over about 18 months, spending more time in hospital than out.

"I still feel very lucky," she said, when asked about those days.

After cancer, Sienna lived an active life.

She went to school, made friends and started dancing — jazz, tap, ballet, she loved them all.

"I lived very normally," she said.

Every six months she would return to hospital for check-ups and when she was 11, she started struggling to breathe during exercise.

Scans revealed her heart was "working under half of what it should", a legacy of the chemotherapy she needed to combat the cancer.

Her mum Lisa wept at the news her youngest child was sick again, but Sienna was resilient.

Sienna Howkins's medication she takes after her heart transplant last year. (ABC News: Luke Bowden)

"I'm very much: this is what it is. I'm just going to deal with it,'" she said. "I just felt like I'd been through cancer and I was fine and I felt like this was just another thing to get through."

Sienna was given medication to stabilise her heart failure and continued dancing.

But in her last year of school, she started deteriorating again.

"I was just tired all the time and I'm very goal driven," she said. "It's so hard wanting to do something and your body's really fighting against you.

"I became really sick, really quickly."

Holding on, staying positive

She started a nursing degree at Griffith University and was listed on the transplant list in her second year.

Five months later, she was at the Logan Hospital emergency department "not feeling great" because of her heart failure, when she got the call from The Prince Charles Hospital that a donor heart had become available.

Sienna Howkins is approaching the one-year anniversary of her transplant. (ABC News: Luke Bowden)

By that stage, although she continued to study part-time, she was spending much of her days sleeping.

"I wasn't living anywhere near how I wanted to live," Sienna said.

"I couldn't function as a regular person. All of my friends were going out — as you do at the age of 19 — clubbing, and this, that, and the other, and I couldn't.

" I knew I had to hold on and just keep positive. "

Before she was wheeled in for her heart transplant, she recalls members of her family crying and "being very scared".

"I'm like: I'm all right. This is a good thing. I'm going to be fine," Sienna said.

"I knew I'd be all right."

Sienna Howkins says she felt "almost euphoric" when she woke up in hospital. (Supplied)

She woke up in the intensive care unit (ICU) feeling "almost euphoric".

"I already felt better and less dizzy," she said. "I felt great.

"With the one kidney … I did have to get quite a bit of dialysis after the transplant, and I had a bit of fluid on me. But apart from that, nothing could have gone better."

As she prepares to mark the one-year anniversary of her transplant, she is looking forward to trips to Bali and New Zealand later this year.

Sienna Howkins (centre) has started dancing again since recieving a heart transplant. (Supplied)

She is playing netball, has returned to dancing and plans to graduate as a nurse at the end of next year, tossing up between specialising in paediatric oncology or ICU nursing.

"What my nurses meant to me and what they've been able to do for me is just crazy," she said.

" They just become part of your family almost. "

Rather than resent her health challenges, Sienna is grateful.

"I'm actually almost thankful for being unwell," she said.

"In a way that's made me who I am. It's definitely changed me. Growing up, I always found I'm a little bit more mature outlook-wise and life-wise than people the same age as me."

Sienna Howkins says she is "almost grateful" for her health challenges. (ABC News: Luke Bowden)

Sienna was one of 16 people who received a heart transplant in Queensland last year.

New DonateLife data, to be released today, shows 557 people donated organs after their deaths in Australia last year — a 6 per cent increase on 2024.

They donated organs to 1,438 Australians — an 8 per cent rise in transplantations.

In 2025, 53 per cent of families consented to organ donation after the death of a relative in hospital — below the peak consent rate of 60 per cent in 2018.

About 35 per cent of eligible Australians are registered to be organ donors.

But only about 2 per cent of people who die in Australian hospitals — or fewer than 1,700 in 2025 — meet the criteria required to be an organ donor.

At the same time, about 2,000 Australians are on a waitlist for an organ transplant.

Living 'life to the fullest' for donor

Sienna Howkins, 20, with her dog Daisy. (ABC News: Luke Bowden)

Sienna said she was determined to "live my life to the fullest for my donor", and recently wrote to their family.

"I gave them an update on what I'm doing with my life," she said.

"I asked them what their loved one liked to do and hopefully, they'll let me know so … I can involve myself with something my donor would have liked."

Sienna Howkins recently wrote to her donor's family to update them on her life. (ABC News: Luke Bowden)

Other than a nursing career and some travelling, Sienna is "just letting the world decide for me" in terms of what the immediate future holds.

"I finally have one again," she said.

To join the Australian organ and tissue donation registry: donatelife.gov.au

title: WA government fights to keep GST deal, seeks 25 per cent mining carve-out
text:
The WA government has stepped up its fight to keep the GST deal it struck with then prime minister Scott Morrison in 2018, arguing that changes would discourage investment.

The Productivity Commission is reviewing the nation's GST arrangement and was due to make recommendations to the federal government next year.

Under a 2018 deal, Western Australia was guaranteed a greater share of the Goods and Services Tax pool than it would otherwise get.

Both parties fear the Vengeful Diety Of The West The GST deal has been described as the worst public policy decision this century, but none of the major parties will touch it for fear of upsetting Western Australia.

The state was forecast to receive $7.8 billion in GST payments this financial year, or about 8 per cent of the total pool, but without the 2018 changes, it would have got just $1.7 billion.

To compensate for WA's increased payments, the Commonwealth has topped up other states to ensure they were no worse off, which has caused a more than $44 billion cost blowout over 10 years.

Western Australia established a GST "fairness fighter" team to mount its case during the inquiry, and spent $1 million on advertising its argument across the country.

WA contributes $32 billion

In its submission to the inquiry, WA has argued Australia should move from full horizontal fiscal equalisation (HFE) — which aims to create a level playing field across all states by compensating for areas with less earning capacity or higher costs — to partial HFE.

How does GST work? Goods and services tax (GST) is a broad-based tax of 10% on most goods, services and other items sold or consumed in Australia.

The Australian Government helps put state governments in a position to provide their residents with comparable services by distributing revenue from the goods and services tax pool according to need.

It is the job of the Commonwealth Grants Commission to advise the Australian Government on how the GST pool should be distributed. Source: Australian Taxation Office and Commonwealth Grants Commission.

That would allow "states like Western Australia to continue investing in their economies, by allowing them to retain more of the benefits of policy decisions and investments", the submission said.

It said other countries had adopted a partial HFE.

But economist Saul Eslake said that did not make it a good idea.

"We are a better country than the other federations in part because we do HFE more comprehensively," Mr Eslake said, pointing to smaller gaps in living standards between Australian states.

Saul Eslake has long been a critic of WA's GST deal. (ABC News: Maren Preuss)

WA's submission estimated since 1942-43, WA had made a net contribution of $32 billion to the federation.

It argued the previous GST system had "many perverse" outcomes, including that "New South Wales, Victoria and Queensland effectively received more of our iron ore, lithium and nickel royalties than we did".

"In contrast, New South Wales effectively retained the vast majority of its coal royalties," the submission reads.

Cost blowouts from the no worse off guarantee were because of higher-than-expected iron ore prices which had benefited the bottom line of the federal budget, according to the submission.

The state government has spent $1 million on an advertising campaign about WA's GST deal. (Supplied)

"Indeed, we estimate that the benefit to the Commonwealth budget through increased company tax revenue alone is three to four times greater than the increased cost of the [guarantee]," it reads.

For those reasons — and many more argued across a 124-page submission — WA Treasurer Rita Saffioti said the current deal should, at the very least, be maintained.

Rita Saffioti has warned against "undermining" the WA economy. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

Partial mining exemption sought

WA wants a 25 per cent reduction on the value of mining revenue which factors into the GST distribution if changes are to be made, to "improve states' incentives to grow their mining industries".

That would halve the cost of the no worse off guarantee in the coming financial year and potentially eliminate the need for it by the end of the decade, it says.

Government vows to defend WA's GST deal When it comes to Western Australia's slice of the GST pie, the state government is making no apologies for ruffling the feathers of other jurisdictions.

Asked why Western Australia was arguing it should provide less support to less prosperous states, Ms Saffioti said partial equalisation allowed for greater "fairness" in the tax system.

"We deliver about 17 per cent of national economic growth," Ms Saffioti said.

"We deliver 50 per cent of national exports.

" You undermine the WA economy, you undermine the national economy. "

Victorian premier weighs in

Across the Nullarbor, Victorian Premier Jacinta Allan seemed unconvinced by WA's case, saying she would "always advocate for Victoria to get its fair share".

"We still collect less revenue per head of person than Western Australia," Ms Allan said.

Jacinta Allan says she won't stop fighting for her state to get more from the deal. (ABC News)

"We are a bigger state, a growing state, a state that is building the infrastructure for the future and delivering the services that working people and family need for our public health and public education systems.

"So that's why I will continue to push for Victoria to get its fair share."

title: Live: 'I feel sorry for him': Young Knights star goes down with non-contact injury
text:
Straight after Sharpe's injury, the Cowboys get a penalty and immediately send big Heilum Luki, playing his first game since 2024, at the injured five-eighth.

And, sure enough, the big back rower storms over almost untouched.

"I feel sorry for Fletcher Sharpe here, because he's not right, he could hardly move," Braith Anasta says on Fox.

"The trainer's given it the all clear when he shouldn't have and it's cost them a try.

"Get him off and reassess him. The kid could hardly get off his tryline. He's in extreme pain."

He leaves the field straight after the try is scored and, making the decision to keep him on the field sillier, on comes overqualified replacement Sandon Smith from the Roosters.

title: Respite care gives Meika time to breathe
text:
Each Wednesday, Meika Robertson's husband Rob spends the day in respite care going on outings, doing puzzles, arts activities and playing guitar.

Rob, a retired musician, enjoys the social interactions and the activities on offer; he's always up for a good natter.

But, perhaps more importantly, while Rob is spending the day away from home, Meika is given a chance to catch her breath.

"It means everything to me," she said.

"It's wonderful because I can have all those hours of doing whatever I need to do. If I need to catch up on jobs I can, or I can just come home and breathe.

"I can do anything that I'm missing out on too. I could go and have coffee with friends. It's just like a release valve for me."

Meika Robertson cares for her husband Rob who has Alzheimer’s. (ABC News: James Tugwell)

Rob was diagnosed with Alzheimer's eight years ago, and while he can still do some self-care things for himself, like dressing and showering, the 74-year-old is no longer able to cook, clean, drive or make decisions for himself — including what to wear for the weather. That all falls to Meika.

But it wasn't until about two years ago that Meika realised she herself needed support in order to keep caring for her husband.

Rob enjoys jam sessions with Carers ACT group team leader Inoeg Nugraha at the day respite centre in Spence. (ABC News: James Tugwell)

"You don't know because you're in it the whole time, so you're just continuing on and on and on, and the stress is building up and up and up," she said.

"Now I'm understanding that there is carer burnout and I've been in a black hole a few times thinking 'I just can't do this anymore'.

"But acknowledging that this is not able to continue at this rate and then having those [respite] services, it's like, 'OK, we'll recharge now, and we can get back to it'."

Carer advocates have long championed the importance of respite care and support for carers, but services can be hard to find.

Demand outstripping supply

There are an estimated 50,000 carers in Canberra, and Carers ACT says demand for respite services far outstrips supply.

"Canberrans are the least likely to be able to access respite of any jurisdiction in Australia and that's just because of the amount of places versus demand," Carers ACT CEO Cain Beckett said.

Carers have also told the ABC that finding and navigating what services were available in the capital was not easy to do.

Cain Beckett says demand for carer respite services in Canberra far outstrips supply. (ABC News: Ruby Thomas)

Carers ACT recently opened the respite program that Rob attends in Spence, and also runs two 'cottages' in Crace and Isaacs that each have four bedrooms offering 24-hour respite services.



Other services in Canberra include those run by Dementia Australia, which offers group and one-on-one respite services each weekday, with participants taking part in arts activities, outings, exercise, and community volunteering.

The ACT government-owned Salvation Army-operated Burrangiri Aged Care Respite Centre has 15 beds offering overnight respite — its fate remains uncertain after being given a two-year lifeline last May.

The Carers ACT day respite centre in Spence offers onsite and offsite activities for participants. (ABC News: James Tugwell)

Mr Beckett said the majority of respite services in Canberra were designed for elderly or frail patients with lower-care needs, and few catered for those with complex or clinical needs.

"If you've got complex care or clinical care needs, there really is often nothing available," he said.

Mr Beckett said the funding framework for overnight respite required change, and aged-care providers needed to be incentivised to offer short-term respite care.

"We need a diversity of different types of respite," Mr Beckett said.



"One of the challenges is that the funding for someone with high-care needs is the same as the funding for someone with lower-care needs, and that means that providers are really incentivised to look after the less complicated cases first."

Respite services 'really critical'

Judy Gordon nursed her mother before she died, and is now caring for her sister.

"My sister can't live alone. I help her with communication and guidance on what to wear, decision-making — she can't make any sort of life decisions," she said.

Judy Gordon says she utilises the day respite service so she can keep fit and healthy and stay socially connected. (ABC News: Ruby Thomas)

Before finding the Carers ACT's day respite service, Ms Gordon said she was "very socially isolated".



"My life before was that I couldn't go and visit my friends, I couldn't maintain friendships — social connection was just fractured," she said.

Now, when her sister is in respite care, Ms Gordon has the opportunity to spend time with her friends.

"And I keep fit. I need to keep fit and healthy, otherwise what's going to happen to her?," she said.

"That's part of my objectives in using the service, to ensure that I keep fit and healthy, and socially connected."

Minister pivots on decision to close Canberra respite service Canberra's Burrangiri Aged Care Respite Centre will stay open.

Such self-care was essential for preventing carer burnout, Mr Beckett said.

"Carers tend to prioritise others' needs before their own, and so sometimes they need a break," he said.

"[Respite care] builds that resilience and wellbeing and allows carers to keep going."

Mr Beckett said carers held the health system together.

"Carers deliver more supports than the entire ACT hospital system every day and it's their work looking after people at home that makes the health system sustainable," he said.

Calls for 'urgent review'

In its budget submission to the ACT government, Carers ACT paints a grim picture of respite in the capital.

"The demand is inexorable," Mr Beckett said.

"It's not something that is new. We've been talking with the ACT and federal governments for a number of years about the need to solve this problem.

"It does continue to grow as more people are supported to age at home and the national aged care reforms, but it's something we really need to get on to and it's a complex problem."

Tanya Buchanan says a lack of access to respite care continues to be a significant issue. (ABC News: Abubakr Sajid )

Dementia Australia has also made a pre-budget submission to the federal government calling for an "urgent independent review of respite services" to improve access and affordability.

"Lack of access to various forms of respite care nationwide has been, and continues to be, a significant issue," Dementia Australia CEO Tanya Buchanan said.

"Limited respite availability, particularly emergency and overnight respite, has been identified ... as an ongoing issue.

" Carers also tell us about the impact lack of respite services can have on their own physical and mental health. "

Rachel Stephen-Smith says the ACT is working with the federal government to expand respite services in the capital. (ABC News: Ruby Thomas)

ACT Health Minister Rachel Stephen-Smith acknowledged that respite care had been a "challenge" for some time, but noted that it was primarily a federal government responsibility.

As part of its re-election pitch, federal Labor promised $10 million for respite services in the ACT.

“We know that respite is something that takes pressure off our services and that it is something the local community really cares about," Ms Stephen-Smith said.



“So we’ve been working with the Commonwealth on its $10 million commitment to expanding respite services.”

There are an estimated 50,000 carers in Canberra. (ABC News: James Tugwell)

Ms Stephen-Smith said the ACT government would also continue to work with providers like Carers ACT to deliver a more diverse range of respite options.

"Particularly for the infrastructure side, there are things that we can do to help, either with land or with our existing facilities that are appropriate to be refurbished for this purpose," she said.

Seek support 'the earlier the better'

Rob and Meika Robertson in their younger years. Rob and Meika once had a recording studio where they created the music for the Australian animated series The Shapies. (Supplied: Meika Robertson)

While some days are more challenging than others, Meika said they were coping and had a "good life", thanks in no small part to the respite services Rob attended.

"We laugh a lot. We're happy people," she said.

"Respite gives me the opportunity to have other conversations with other people to keep my sense of worth, if you will, but generally we have good days."

Meik says she and Rob have a "good life" and enjoy sharing a laugh. (ABC News: James Tugwell)

She encouraged other carers to seek support sooner rather than later.

"The earlier the better really … to get into a pattern so that you know your partner, or whoever, will enjoy those things and you will be able to survive," she said.

title: 'Fill up your car' before petrol price spikes, analyst says
text:
The world is bracing for a surge in the oil price when commodities markets open tomorrow, with reports the air war between the US, Israel and Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz to shipping.

The conflict has erupted around the strait, which is a critical shipping route connecting the region's biggest oil producers to the world.

The region, including Saudia Arabia, Iraq, Iran, UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain and Oman, produces 27 per cent of the world's crude oil.

About three-quarters of that (about 20 per cent of the world's total oil supply) passes through the Strait of Hormuz.

A smaller proportion passes through the Red Sea and the narrow Bab-el-Mandeb Strait.

Iran fires a missile during exercises near the Strait of Hormuz in 2022. (Iranian Army/West Asia News Agency/Reuters)

While no country has exclusive control of the Strait of Hormuz, Iran is a threatening military presence and could exercise sovereignty 12 nautical miles from its coastline, restricting shipping at the narrowest point.

Vessels in the area have received radio transmissions alerting them that the Strait of Hormuz is closed, the UK Maritime Trade Operations agency said.

The US Department of Transportation Maritime Administration also issued an alert: "It is recommended that vessels keep clear of this area if possible."

There has been no official word from Iran that it intends to close the Strait.

What we know about the strikes on Iran After weeks of anticipation Israel fired the first shot on Iran, drawing in the US and sparking retaliatory strikes that have hit several countries.

"Oil prices will spike sharply, possibly above $US100 a barrel, from $US67 on Friday for West Texas, given the disruption to oil supplies, including via the Strait of Hormuz," AMP chief economist Shane Oliver told the ABC.

"This is particularly so given the broad nature of the US and Israeli attack — including talk of regime change — and Iran’s broad-based retaliation so far.

"The key issue is how long it lasts."

Mr Oliver said a prolonged conflict would "cause more damage economically".

'Fill up your car' before petrol price spikes

The oil price spiked above $US120 a barrel in 2022 when Russia invade Ukraine, but economists say this conflict could be more significant given the key oil producers involved.

Marcus Today senior market analyst Henry Jennings agreed with Mr Oliver.

"If the straits are blocked, we could see $US90 oil on a spike, so it may be wise to fill up your car with some cheap petrol in the next few days," Mr Jennings said.

"Much depends on the Iranian reaction."

Analysts say a $US10-a-barrel rise roughly translates to a cent a litre in Australian petrol prices.

So a rise from $US67 on Friday to $US107 could push local petrol prices up by 40 cents a litre.

The immediate implications of the conflict for the Australian economy are mixed.

"So if there are sustained interruptions to the flow of oil from the Middle East to China, that could have a negative impact on China's economy and, presumably, on China's imports from Australia," Mr Oliver said.

"Also, note that close to one-fifth of the world's LNG also passes through the Strait of Hormuz, so there could be implications for LNG prices, too, which would be a positive for Australia as we are also a big exporter of LNG, although it might also mean higher domestic gas prices."

title: Your complete guide to the Matildas Women's Asian Cup squad
text:
The Matildas are once again hoping to capture the public's attention and support throughout the Women's Asian Cup in March, played across Sydney, Perth, and the Gold Coast.

Here's your handy guide to all squad members, from the superstars like Sam Kerr, Ellie Carpenter, and Mary Fowler, to the rising talent like Amy Sayer, Wini Heatley, and Kahli Johnson.

Get the latest: Listen to ABC Sport for coverage of the 2026 AFC Women's Asian Cup in Australia, and keep up to date online.

Goalkeepers

Goalkeeper: Mackenzie Arnold Mackenzie Arnold. ( ) Name: Mackenzie Arnold Hometown: Gold Coast, QLD Current club: Portland Thorns (USA) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 61 (0) Arnold will be forever remembered for her heroics in Australia’s 2023 World Cup quarterfinal victory over France, in which she made three saves in a mammoth 20-kick penalty shootout. She made her international debut in 2012, and has played for clubs in Australia, England and the US. Arnold also lives with hearing loss and is an advocate for the Deaf and hard of hearing community. Goalkeeper: Morgan Aquino Morgan Aquino. ( ) Name: Morgan Aquino Hometown: Perth, WA Current club: DC Power (USA) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 0 (0) Aquino was a very late addition to the squad, officially replacing the injured Jada Whyman on the day of the Matildas first match. She is the only uncapped player in the squad, and familiar to Perth Glory fans. The 24-year-old now plays for DC Power in the USL Super League, which is another top-tier competition in the USA, though less established than the NWSL. Goalkeeper: Chloe Lincoln Chloe Lincoln. ( ) Name: Chloe Lincoln Hometown: Armidale, NSW Current club: Brisbane Roar (Australia) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 3 (0) Lincoln missed initial selection for the 2026 Asian Cup and is only part of the Australian squad due to the late withdrawal of Teagan Micah. The 21-year-old made her international debut in 2024 and has spent her entire career in the A-League, representing Canberra United, Western United and Brisbane Roar, with whom she signed with before the 2025/26 season.

Defenders

Defender: Clare Hunt Clare Hunt. ( ) Name: Clare Hunt Hometown: Grenfell, NSW Current club: Tottenham Hotspur (England) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 39 (1) Hunt's rise to Matildas stardom was rapid, progressing from an international debutant in February 2023 to being a key member of Australia's defence at the World Cup later that year. She has gone from strength to strength since then, signing for PSG in the months after the tournament and then Tottenham, for whom she is a first team regular in the Women's Super League. Defender: Steph Catley Steph Catley. ( ) Name: Steph Catley Hometown: Melbourne, VIC Current club: Arsenal (England) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 141 (7) A veteran of three World Cups and the fifth most capped Matilda of all-time, Catley is a legend of Australian football. Having spent most of her career at fullback, Catley has moved more centrally in recent seasons. She has enjoyed considerable success with club side Arsenal, winning last season’s UEFA Champions League and two FA Cups before that. Before her switch to English football, Catley represented teams in Australia and the US. Defender: Alanna Kennedy Alanna Kennedy. ( ) Name: Alanna Kennedy Hometown: Sydney, NSW Current club: London City Lionesses (England) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 142 (12) Another Matildas stalwart, Kennedy has forged a career as a tough tackling, no-nonsense centre-half. She made her international debut in 2012 as a 17-year-old and has gone on to grace three World Cups and this will be her fourth Asian Cup. Kennedy has played for a host of clubs in Australia, England as well as the US, and signed for current club London City at the beginning of the 2025/26 season. Defender: Winonah Heatley Winonah Heatley. ( ) Name: Winonah Heatley Hometown: Port Douglas, QLD Current club: AS Roma (Italy) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 13 (0) On the fringes of the team since her first Matildas call-up in 2021, the 2026 Asian Cup promises to be a breakout tournament for Heatley. Capable of playing across the back line or in holding midfield, Heatley impressed with Växjö DFF in Sweden and FC Nordsjælland in Denmark before signing for Italian giants AS Roma in 2025. Defender: Charlize Rule Charlize Rule. ( ) Name: Charlize Rule Hometown: Sydney, NSW Current club: Brighton and Hove Albion (England) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 1 (0) Capable of playing in the centre on the right of a back four, Rule could prove a valuable utility player for the Matildas at the 2026 Asian Cup. She made her international debut in December 2023, before injury kept her out of action for more than a year. Rule was an A-League winner with Sydney FC before moving to England in 2023. Defender: Courtney Nevin Courtney Nevin. ( ) Name: Courtney Nevin Hometown: Sydney, NSW Current club: Malmo FF (Sweden) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 40 (1) Still just 24 years old, Nevin has been in and around the Matildas set-up since making her international debut in 2021. An energetic and attacking fullback, Nevin has Olympic and World Cup experience and a likely starter for the Matildas at the 2026 Asian Cup. She represented teams in Australia, Denmark and England before signing for Swedish club Malmo FF last year. Defender: Ellie Carpenter Ellie Carpenter. ( ) Name: Ellie Carpenter Hometown: Cowra, NSW Current club: Chelsea (England) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 93 (5) In 2016, Carpenter became the first player born after 2000 to play for the Matildas. Since then, she has become one of the team’s most recognisable faces and consistent contributors. The 25-year-old is the quintessential fullback – hardworking, adventurous and reliable, forging a glittering career for top clubs in Australia, the US, France and now England. Defender: Jamilla Rankin Jamilla Rankin. Name: Jamilla Rankin Hometown: Eureka, NSW Current club: TSG Hoffenheim (Germany) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 7 (0) A central defender by trade, Rankin began her footballing journey in the tiny Northern NSW town of Eureka. She represented both Brisbane Roar and Melbourne Victory in the A-League before making the move to Europe with TSG Hoffenheim, where she has become a regular for the German club. Since debuting in a 7-0 loss to Spain in 2022, Rankine has been on the fringes of the national-team set-up and will be a rotation option at the 2026 Asian Cup.

Midfielders

Midfielder: Clare Wheeler Clare Wheeler. ( ) Name: Clare Wheeler Hometown: Newcastle, NSW Current club: Everton (England) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 34 (2) Wheeler is a hardworking and technically sound midfielder, most comfortable as a number six but also capable of playing a box-to-box role. She was included in the Matildas squad for the 2023 World Cup but didn't get on the pitch. Since then, she has developed into a key figure at Women's Super League side Everton and will likely be a starter in the Matildas' engine room at the Asian Cup. Midfielder: Alex Chidiac Alex Chidiac of the Matildas attempts a shot at goal during the Cup of Nations match between the Australia Matildas and Jamaica in Newcastle on February 22, 2023. ( ) Name: Alex Chidiac Hometown: Adelaide, SA Current club: FC Como (Italy) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 37 (2) An international debutant at just 16, Chidiac has been in and around the Matildas set-up for over a decade, without ever truly nailing down a starting spot. However, the versatile midfielder has undeniable quality. Last year, she became just the fifth player to claim a second Julie Dolan Medal (the award given to the A-League Women's best player) and has made an impressive start to life with Italian side Como, which she joined in mid-2025. Midfielder: Kyra Cooney-Cross Kyra Cooney-Cross. ( ) Name: Kyra Cooney-Cross Hometown: Sunshine Coast, QLD Current club: Arsenal (England) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 63 (2) Cooney-Cross only scores bangers. Responsible for remarkable long-range strikes against Germany in 2024 and then New Zealand late last year, the silky and stylish 24-year-old is a hub of creativity in the Matildas' midfield and one of the first names on the team sheet. After a couple seasons on the fringes of a very strong Arsenal team, she has seen more game time with Gunners this season. Earlier this year, the Australian football community rallied around Cooney-Cross after her mother was diagnosed with a rare and incurable form of cancer. Midfielder: Katrina Gorry Katrina Gorry. ( ) Name: Katrina Gorry Hometown: Brisbane, QLD Current club: West Ham United (England) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 118 (18) Stalwart, leader, legend – "Mini" has been a fixture of the Matildas' midfield for well over a decade. But, having debuted two years after Australia’s breakthrough Asian Cup triumph in 2010, is yet to claim a trophy in green and gold. Gorry's globetrotting career has seen her feature for 10 clubs across three continents and rewarded her with a trio of World Cup appearances. Her hard running and tenacity will be vital for Montemurro at the Asian Cup. Midfielder: Amy Sayer Amy Sayer. ( ) Name: Amy Sayer Hometown: Sydney, NSW Current club: Malmo FF (Sweden) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 19 (4) If you ever want to feel your own achievements fade into insignificance, cast an eye over Sayer's CV. The attacking midfielder graduated from Stanford with a degree in human biology and philosophy after achieving a perfect score in her ATAR, all while establishing herself as one of Australian football's brightest prospects. However, it hasn't all been sunshine and rainbows for the 24-year-old, with an ACL injury keeping her off the pitch for over a year and robbing her of a berth at the 2024 Olympics. Midfielder: Emily van Egmond Emily van Egmond. ( ) Name: Emily Van Egmond Hometown: Newcastle, NSW Current club: Leicester City (England) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 165 (32) Another of the well established Matildas crew with the most international caps out of anyone in the squad. Perhaps no longer the force she once was, supplanted by some of the younger crop coming through in the midfield. But she continues to prove that she's capable of making an impact when needed, and her experience will count for plenty when it comes to the big moments.

Forwards

Forward: Caitlin Foord Caitlin Foord. ( ) Name: Caitlin Foord Hometown: Shellharbour, NSW Current club: Arsenal (England) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 140 (39) One of the Matildas' formidable front three, Caitlin Foord remains an electrifying, world-class talent. She made her international debut as a 16-year-old in 2011, and at 31 remains a Matildas linchpin. Sometimes overshadowed in the headlines by Sam Kerr and Mary Fowler, but Foord is one of the most crucial players in the side, and consistently devastating on the international stage. Forward: Mary Fowler Mary Fowler. ( ) Name: Mary Fowler Hometown: Cairns, QLD Current club: Manchester City (England) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 63 (16) A crowd favourite and long heralded as having the potential to finish her career as one of Australian football's all-time greats. Her vision is unparalleled — she simply sees things that others don't, and she has the exceptional skill to make magic happen. She released a book last year where she revealed many challenges she's faced through her career, including mental health struggles, and racism. She spent most of last year sidelined with a ruptured ACL, but returned for club side Manchester City at the beginning of February. Coach Joe Montemurro says she's fully fit, and if the Matildas make a deep run in the tournament, no doubt Fowler will be a key reason why. Forward: Hayley Raso Hayley Raso. ( ) Name: Hayley Raso Hometown: Gold Coast, QLD Current Club: Eintracht Frankfurt (Germany) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 103 (24) An electrifying winger who is unrelenting and has pace to burn, Raso is another of the fierce front three alongside Kerr and Foord. She's been somewhat of a journey-woman in club football, with stints in Australia, the USA, England, Spain and Germany, but always amongst the first picked for national duty. Forward: Michelle Heyman Michelle Heyman. ( ) Name: Michelle Heyman Hometown: Shellharbour, NSW Current club: Canberra United (Australia) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 85 (33) This is the story that keeps on giving. In 2019, Heyman retired from international football. And as the Tokyo Olympics and the 2023 Women's World Cup came and went, there was no inkling a comeback was on the cards. But when Sam Kerr injured her ACL, the Matildas were in need of an out and out striker. And Heyman answered the call. Since her return, the 37-year-old played a key role in helping the Matildas qualify for the Paris 2024 Olympics, and she attended her second Games. Her A-League Women form continues to dazzle, and it's no surprise she's earned a spot in this squad. Forward: Kahli Johnson Kahli Johnson. ( ) Name: Kahli Johnson Hometown: Sydney, NSW Current club: Calgary Wild FC (Canada) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 3 (1) This is Johnson's first major tournament and she's the only squad member playing in Canada's Northern Super League. She represented the Young Matildas at the U20 World Cup in 2022 and 2024, and earned her move to Canada after a stand-out season for Western United in the 2024/25 A-League Women season. Forward: Sam Kerr Sam Kerr. ( ) Name: Sam Kerr Hometown: Perth, WA Current club: Chelsea (England) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 131 (69) It's testament to how far the Matildas have come that everything no longer revolves around Sam Kerr. Make no mistake, she's still amongst the world's elite strikers and elevates the Matildas in a way only she can. But the team was forced to evolve during her near two-year absence with an ACL injury. She remains a first-choice player and the captain and is playing in her fifth Asian Cup. She's the only squad member to have played in the 2010 victory, and will be desperate to win this trophy to cement her already enshrined legacy as an Australian football icon. Forward: Holly McNamara Holly McNamara. ( ) Name: Holly McNamara Hometown: Sydney, NSW Current club: Melbourne City (Australia) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 16 (1) One of the feel good stories of this squad. McNamara has had a wretched time with injury, suffering three ACL injuries in a few years — wrecking her hopes of playing in the 2023 Women's World Cup and then the Paris 2024 Olympics. So to see her healthy, and dominating the A-League Women is a sight worth celebrating, and she's proven she can capably lead the line whenever called upon. Forward: Remy Siemsen Remy Siemsen. ( ) Name: Remy Siemsen Hometown: Sydney, NSW Current club: FC Rosengård (Sweden) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 14 (0) Siemsen was crushed to miss selection for the 2023 World Cup, and gets a slice of redemption here. She recently moved to a new club in Sweden, and is a reliable option off the bench if Montemurro needs to call on her through the tournament. Forward: Kaitlyn Torpey Kaitlyn Torpey. ( ) Name: Kaitlyn Torpey Hometown: Brisbane, QLD Current club: Newcastle United (England) Caps/goals before the 2026 Asian Cup: 21 (2) Torpey is listed in the forwards, but may end up being used as a wing-back through this tournament. Her versatility is her strength and she's made the most of the opportunities she's been given on the international stage so far.

Coach

title: Chef tackles food waste by cooking 'funny' meat cuts
text:
When chef Dom Aboud had to order a dozen Scotch fillets for his restaurant, he started thinking about the other 200 kilograms of meat on the animal.

So he decided to buy a whole steer.

"If I just said I wanted 12 Scotch fillets, they're going to be left with something like 200 kilos of meat to move," Aboud said.

Dom Aboud says Chianina beef was well received in his restaurant. (ABC Central West: Emily Middleton)

" From a sustainability and chef's sense, I don't want to see great produce go in the bin and as a business owner, we don't want to see anything go in the bin. "

Aboud is the executive chef at Sienna Hospitality in Orange, Central West New South Wales, which runs local restaurant Union Bank.

He said using the whole carcass was a great way to support local producers and significantly reduce food waste.

"We're getting to the fun part now where we're trying to figure out what to do with all these funny cuts and trim."

Dom Aboud has been wanting to buy a whole steer from a local grower for some time now. (ABC Central West: Emily Middleton)

The whole beast

The carcass arrives whole before it is cut up for the restaurant. (Supplied: Dom Aboud)

High-end restaurants in metropolitan areas have been utilising the whole-animal approach for some time, according to Meat and Livestock Australia (MLA), but it is still considered an uncommon practice.

"You've got an opportunity to use that whole carcass, you're not going to buy it to waste it," MLA's product and business development manager and corporate chef Sam Burke said.

" That means more opportunities to get more beef on the menu. "

Just over 8 per cent of a cow carcass is used for prime cut steaks such as Scotch fillets and sirloins, according to the MLA.

Even the fat was used to make tallow and other sauces. (ABC Central West: Emily Middleton)

The rest can be used for secondary offcuts, which are far less popular on supermarket shelves, and can sometimes be put into pet food.

Burke said the trend of restaurants opting to purchase an entire carcass was slowly growing.

He said using a whole carcass made financial sense, as chefs could utilise 59 per cent more meat.

"It provides an efficiency dividend on cost, you're getting a lower per kilo price.

" It encourages the chef to be more creative, which is very exciting with Australian beef on the menu, and it lends itself to sustainable eating. "

Tilda Chianina provided the restaurant with its first steer. (Supplied: Jason Ryan)

What's on the menu?

Among the dishes Aboud is now cooking are a Lebanese-inspired corned beef, veal schnitzel and a chuck roll using meat usually processed into mince.

He said he also was discovering unusual cuts that customers were loving.

"Finding things like a Sierra steak in the shoulder muscle, which is a really long flank-looking steak that grills up beautifully and would usually get trimmed out and tossed to the side.

" We've put all these things on the menu; people have been literally eating it up. It's been really, really good so far. "

Aboud said they were ordering a full carcass every four to six weeks.

"Recognising that this is the most win-win way for us to do it was the first thing," he said.

Using the whole beef means exploring different uses for various obscure cuts. (ABC Central West: Emily Middleton)

The approach is something Aboud hopes other restaurants adopt to reduce food waste.

"Australia is such a great place in general for old steak and chips, but levelling it up and really putting that care into using a whole beast and thinking about proactive ways that we can all do it," he said.

" Instead of just ordering boxes and boxes and boxes of sirloin and getting left with everything else that comes with having to slaughter a steer for 12 sirloins. "

Italian-heritage cattle

The restaurant's first full steer came from Tilda Chianina, a property at Cudal, west of Orange.

It's one of just a handful of farms in Australia that carries the Italian Chianina breed.

The breed has a distinctive tenderness and leanness according to Jason Ryan, director of Tilda Chianina.

Jason Ryan says the Chianina breed is known for the tenderness of its meat. (Supplied: Jason Ryan)

"They're the largest and heaviest cattle in the world … they're a very old historic breed, originally designed to pull ploughs."

The most famous cut associated with the Chianina is the Bistecca alla Fiorentina, or Florentine steak.

Chianina beef is most commonly known for the T-bone or Florentine steak. (ABC Central West: Emily Middleton)

"You could probably just about count the number of Chianina breeders on one hand," Mr Ryan said.

This isn't the first time a restaurant has purchased a whole steer from him.

Mr Ryan said while many other high-end restaurants had done it before, it was an approach his business opted for after watching the rise and fall of cattle markets.

"We don't have the scale to ride the highs and lows of a normal sort of unpredictable market," he said.

The Chianina breed is known for being quite large, sometimes standing 2 metres tall. (Supplied: Jason Ryan)

"We want to look after and use the whole beast in our operation.

"We can't just look at primal cuts and things like that … it's great when the likes of Dom and restaurants get behind that type of approach and really showcase how you can use a whole beast."

title: Sri Lanka helps New Zealand into World Cup semis despite Pakistan defeat
text:
New Zealand has been left celebrating in the team hotel as it watched Pakistan fall short in its bold bid to pinch a place in the T20 World Cup semifinals.

Pakistan needed to conjure up a 65-run victory or better against co-hosts Sri Lanka in Pallekele to qualify ahead of the Black Caps but though it amassed a huge 8-212, headed by a record-breaking ton from Sahibzada Farhan, it ultimately could only manage a five-run victory in a thrilling conclusion.

Yet there must have been some nervous moments for New Zealand skipper Mitch Santner and his team as they watched the drama unfold on television back in Colombo as Pakistan tried to restrict Sri Lanka to a total of 147 or less that would have eliminated the Kiwis.

When Sri Lanka fell to 5-101 off 12 overs it still looked feasible that Pakistan could pull it off, but a magnificent counter-attack by Pavan Rathnayake, who crashed a 37-ball 58, ensured its dream was buried once Sri Lanka motored to 147 in the 16th over.

More drama was still to come as Sri Lanka, already eliminated from its own tournament, launched one final heroic attempt to end a disappointing campaign with a glorious victory, led by captain Dasun Shanaka.

Needing 28 from the last over bowled by Pakistan's star quick Shaheen Shah Afridi, Shanaka sent the crowd into a frenzy as he smoked the first four balls for 4, 6, 6 and 6.

Sri Lanka needed six from the final two balls, but on the first the skipper attempted a hugely ambitious reverse scoop that didn't make contact and then he left the final delivery, feeling sure Afridi's slanted yorker would be called wide.

It was deemed a legal delivery though, leaving Shanaka unbeaten on 76 out of Sri Lanka's 6-207, having clouted eight sixes in his remarkable 31-ball effort.

Earlier Pakistan, who'd been put in to bat by Shanaka, had been prepared to gamble by dropping superstar Babar Azam and thrusting Fakhar Zaman up the order.

Loading Instagram content

Along with their star of the tournament Farhan, Fakhar smashed the bowling to all parts of the Pallekele International Stadium as the pair racked up a remarkable opening partnership of 176 in just 16.5 overs.

The 29-year-old late bloomer Farhan enjoyed another landmark day, going past Virat Kohli's 12-year-old record of 319 runs in a single T20 World Cup before becoming the first man to score two tons in one edition.

While Fakhar eventually fell for 84, with nine fours and two sixes, Farhan went on to three-figures off 59 balls.

Pakistan couldn't keep up the momentum as wickets tumbled swiftly. None of its other batters could reach double figures as they eked out 14 for the loss of five wickets over the last couple of overs.

AAP

title: Beachgoers mesmerised by 1,100kg bull taking a stroll
text:
A bull casually strolling along Albany's pristine coastline is not an expected sight for many beachgoers.

But at Nanarup Beach, 435 kilometres south of Perth, it is just another day in the life of local legend Bruce the Brahman.

The 1,100-kilogram bull has called Albany home since 2018, rescued and rehomed by former Kalgan Rodeo organiser Tom Kennedy, who said Bruce would have faced an uncertain fate without him.

Tom Kennedy has looked after Bruce since he was a tiny calf. (ABC Great Southern: Kate Forrester)

"I wanted a little fella that kids could actually meet … a real bull," Mr Kennedy said.

"He had no purpose at his old stud and was about to be sent to the abattoir, so I decided to take him in.

" It was all about showing people just how inquisitive and intelligent the Brahman breed really is. "

A pet for the whole town

Almost a decade after the Kennedy family took him in as a pet, Bruce the bull has become a local celebrity in Albany.

He is often spotted riding in the back of Mr Kennedy’s truck, visiting various spots around the Great Southern town.

Tom Kennedy carefully loads Bruce onto his trailer, gearing up for a fun-filled day at the beach. (ABC Great Southern: Kate Forrester)

"He visits the bowling alley in town, I've taken him with me as I've been horse-riding through the bush, he's gone to the Boddington rodeo — all over the place," Mr Kennedy said.

"You simply just ask him, 'Do you want to go for a ride on the truck?', and he will just wander up to it and get on."

Loading...

Mr Kennedy said people were often curious about the breed.

"People are often mesmerised by the animal — a lot ask, 'What's its hump for?'" he said.

"They're bred to resist ticks and require less food and water, so they can thrive in warmer climates, like in northern Australia."

Brahmans are a common cattle breed in Queensland, north-eastern Western Australia, the Northern Territory, and the north coast of New South Wales. (ABC Rural: Lara Webster)

An online star

In recent years, Bruce, who is also affectionately known as "Brucey", has become something of a social media influencer, attracting visitors from beyond Albany for a meet and greet.

Amanda Thomson said she had followed Bruce's adventures from the beginning.

"I grew up on a farm in Many Peaks, near Albany, with cows and bulls — and you wouldn't go anywhere near them," Ms Thomson said.

Amanda Thomson says she has dreamed of meeting Bruce the Brahman, having followed his adventures on social media. (ABC Great Southern: Kate Forrester)

"At first, I was dubious to approach Brucey, but then you just fall in love with him."

Mr Kennedy said that taking Bruce to different places was about much more than just meeting a bull.

"People right now need something simple and real to smile at, I think, and Bruce just has the ability to make people happy and laugh," he said.

Beachgoers are taken aback when they spot a bull walking along the shoreline. (ABC Great Southern: Kate Forrester)

The celebrity bull has even landed his own book deal.

"An author from over east saw a video of Bruce chasing jetskis and contacted me about writing a story on him," Mr Kennedy said.

"She came over to meet him — so I guess that is the next chapter for Brucey!"

title: Record rain for parts of SA
text:
Record rain has fallen in parts of South Australia, bringing relief to some in regional areas after long dry spells — with authorities preparing for more heavy rain in parts of the state on Sunday.

It follows heavy rain in the west of the state, with 75 millimetres at Ceduna, 121mm at Wirrulla and 103mm at Minnipa.

For James Kerr, who runs 100,000-hectare pastoral station Buckleboo on the Eyre Peninsula, the rain could not have come at a better time.

Down to his last dam water on Thursday, Mr Kerr's property received 50mm overnight, half-filling his 35 catchments.

"The timing is impeccable," Mr Kerr said.

"If we didn't get the rain this weekend, we'd have to move the sheep next week out onto the paddock where we had the bore and that would have been the last water we had on the property.

"This year we were staring down the barrel of another year with no grass rain.

"For our summer active grasses, this is the first drink they've had in three summers."

Power disrupted at Flinders Medical Centre amid heavy rain A temporary power disruption has impacted surgery capacity at one of Adelaide's largest hospitals as parts of the state received record rainfall.

Stacey Horgan, who lives at Poochera, about 140km east of Ceduna, said she had received 150mm from Friday night through to yesterday.

She said she had not seen anything like it in 15 years at Poochera.

"We've got a swimming pool we've had to empty three times because it's overflowing," Ms Horgan said.

"Yeah, it's been incredible to see this much rain."

The MFS responded to a roof collapse at a shop in Renmark. (ABC News: Shannon Pearce)

Other parts of the state have also recorded downpours, including 142mm at Yunta, in South Australia's outback.

In the Riverland, 43mm of rainfall was recorded at Loxton in three hours on Sunday morning.

In nearby Renmark, the Metropolitan Fire Service were called to assist with a roof collapse at a shop.

Heavy rain has fallen in Renmark in South Australia's Riverland. (ABC News: Shannon Pearce)

Check weather conditions, SES urges

Ursula Connor from The Bureau of Meteorology said the low-pressure system was moving "quite quickly" through the pastoral districts.

"It does look like the heaviest falls will be on and east of the Mount Lofty Ranges, so those more significant thunderstorms and showers possibly missing Adelaide. But we're still expecting fairly persistent rain and shower activity through the area — pretty much what we've been seeing over the last day or so," she told ABC Radio's Jason Chong on Sunday morning.

Ms Connor said the heaviest falls would be pushed out towards the eastern border ranges late on Sunday evening and persist into Monday morning, with isolated showers lingering on Tuesday before conditions settled across the state.

"By the time we get to mid-week ... [it's] looking quite dry across most of the state for the rest of the week," she said.

On Saturday, SA SES chief of staff Derren Halleday said the emergency service had planned for "worst-case scenarios" due to unpredictability about where most rain would fall.

A severe weather warning had been in place for the Adelaide metropolitan area early on Sunday morning, however it was later cancelled for that district.

Warnings remain in place for certain parts of the state.

Mr Halleday said Adelaide residents should check the latest on the Bureau of Meteorology website before leaving home.

The SES urged people not to drive, ride or walk through floodwaters and to keep clear of creeks and storm drains.

Mr Halleday said the SES had distributed more than 83,000 sandbags by Saturday afternoon and responded to about 140 requests since Friday.

title: She cared for her ailing husband for years. There was a sting waiting in his will
text:
Advertisement

Exclusive NationalNSWInheritance She cared for her ailing husband for years. There was a sting waiting in his will Michaela Whitbourn March 1, 2026 — 1:51pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Save this article for later Add articles to your saved list and come back to them anytime. Got it Share A A A

A woman who cared for her ailing husband for years before his death, including periods where she stopped work, has lost a court bid to secure a share of his $1.9 million estate. The Victorian Supreme Court decision is the latest in a series of cases across the country in inheritance disputes, and has highlighted the importance of establishing financial need to make a successful claim. The wife cared for her husband for years before his death. She was not included in his will. Artwork: Stephen Kiprillis The man died in May 2024. He had made a will in 2006 that did not name his wife, a doctor in Melbourne, as a beneficiary. He was survived by his daughter from a previous marriage, who was the primary beneficiary under the will.

Advertisement

His wife applied to the Victorian Supreme Court for a family provision order, which is a way for current or former dependants of a deceased, among others, to seek a larger slice of the inheritance pie if inadequate provision was made for them in a will. The courts will make such an order only on a needs basis. “The plaintiff said she was hurt when she found out the deceased had excluded her from his will because she regarded their relationship as great and she had sacrificed a lot to look after the deceased for most of their married life,” Associate Justice Ian Irving said in his decision last month. The daughter applied for the step-mother’s claim to be summarily dismissed – meaning rejected without a full trial – on the basis that it had no prospect of success. ‘The deceased’s rehabilitation took almost one year during which time the plaintiff stopped working to care for him.’ Associate Justice Ian Irving The doctor told the court her financial position was stable. She owned an unencumbered house valued at $2.65 million as at March 31 last year, as well as owning two investment properties valued at a combined $3.1 million.

Advertisement

She also had “superannuation in the form of a further property which pays $25,000 per annum in rent and a term deposit of $300,000,” Irving said. The doctor and her late husband married and started living together in 2006, but his health declined substantially in the following years. She made considerable sacrifices to care for him until his death. “In 2009 the deceased had a stroke which left him, among other things, unable to speak, with double vision and poor balance,” Irving said. “The deceased’s rehabilitation took almost one year during which time the plaintiff stopped working to care for him. The deceased recovered to the extent that he could travel with the plaintiff again but not sufficiently to recommence working.” The wife returned to work but reduced her working hours to continue caring for him. His health deteriorated further from 2011, and she cut her hours of work again.

Advertisement

From 2020 until his death in May 2024, she was his full-time carer. She told the court she was also responsible for caring for her mother who was 101 as of March 31 last year. ‘Unfortunately, the existence of a moral duty alone is not sufficient to found a claim.’ Associate Justice Ian Irving “The plaintiff said that while she is ‘far from destitute’, she ‘sacrificed [her] career and [her] considerable earning capacity’ to care for the deceased when he was unwell,” the judge said. The daughter conceded her step-mother was an eligible person to bring the claim but argued she had no real prospect of success because she has failed to demonstrate any financial or other need.

Advertisement

The judge agreed. “[Notwithstanding] the plaintiff’s strong moral claim, she has not demonstrated that she is unable to adequately provide for her own proper maintenance and support,” he concluded. Related Article Exclusive

Divorce ‘Divorce season’: The surge in couples separating after the holidays “Unfortunately, the existence of a moral duty alone is not sufficient to found a claim. “[While] the plaintiff made continued and considerable sacrifices to look after the deceased and ensure his life was comfortable and enjoyable, her claim for provision has no real prospect of success. “The plaintiff’s absence of need and her comfortable financial position are clear on the face of the evidence and are unlikely to change at trial. The relatively modest size of the estate also means that to allow the matter to proceed further would unnecessarily deplete the estate and delay its administration.”

Advertisement

James Penman, principal of Melbourne law firm Vita Legal, said the case confirmed that proving financial need is essential in a family provision case. “While the court can take into account a wide range of factors, if you can’t prove actual financial need, your claim is going to fail,” Penman said. He said that in family provision cases “the size of the estate is very important – it’s one of the first things you look at when advising a client about this type of claim”. “In this case, however, it wouldn’t have mattered. The outcome would be the same if the estate’s assets were $5 million or $50 million. “The claim failed because the plaintiff had no identifiable financial need. Once that fact was determined by the court, the claim had no chance of success.”

Advertisement

When financial need is demonstrated, the size of the estate is relevant. “There was a recent case in NSW where a plaintiff sued his brother’s estate and got over $1.4m, despite him having a poor relationship with his brother,” Penman said. “The plaintiff’s claim was helped by the fact the estate had $50 million in assets. The judge said that having a large estate allowed the court to ‘make a more liberal assessment’ of what the plaintiff should receive. If the estate was smaller, the plaintiff may have got nothing. But because there was plenty in the estate, he got over a million.” Penman said that “one mistake plaintiffs often make is bringing a family provision claim thinking its purpose is to make things ‘fair’. “The court is not concerned with fairness in these claims, and it will not, and cannot, rewrite someone’s will simply to make things equal between family members.

Advertisement

“Instead, the focus is on whether the plaintiff has been left with ‘adequate provision’ for their proper maintenance and support.” Start the day with a summary of the day’s most important and interesting stories, analysis and insights. Sign up for our Morning Edition newsletter.

title: Family of champion Australian athlete stranded in Middle East amid war chaos
text:
“I’m thinking ‘sitting in an airplane on a tarmac in a major international airport is probably not a good place to be’,” she said. “People were pretty concerned and pretty keen to get off the plane.” The effect the conflict has had on global aviation has so far been dramatic, grounding thousands of flights, forcing passengers to take cover at airports, and throwing route networks into disarray. Airlines have cancelled nearly 2000 flights since the hostilities began on Saturday (AEDT). An Asian national was killed, and seven people were injured at Abu Dhabi’s Zayed International Airport in the UAE, Abu Dhabi Airports reported on X. Emirates media office reported four people were injured during an “incident” at Dubai’s international airport as Iran launches retaliatory strikes. “All the alerts just keep going off, and they go off on a fairly regular basis,” Penni said from Doha airport. “I haven’t worked out whether they coincide with missiles or whether they’re just random.” One of the alerts the Miltons received in Doha airport. The first were only in Arabic. Credit: Penni Milton

The Miltons were supposed to spend a night in Venice with Michael before he would travel to the Athlete’s village in Cortina. They haven’t seen him since mid-January because of his training schedule and injury rehabilitation, but have had to abandon that plan and instead spend a second night in Doha airport with no end in sight. Milton has won 11 medals, including six gold medals, across six Paralympic Games. He won Australia’s first-ever Winter Paralympic gold medal in 1992, before later becoming the fourth Australian to compete at a Summer and Winter Paralympics at Beijing in 2008 in para-cycling. Angus and Matilda were too young to watch Milton compete in his first six Paralympic Games, and having them travel to Italy to watch was a major factor in deciding to make his Paralympic comeback, 20 years on. It was also a significant financial load on the family and Michael, who has self-funded his return to the Games. “I’m sure there’s lots of people in the same boat who have lives planned and this just interrupts the good times they have planned, and they’re definitely not having good times at the moment,” he said from Cortina. “It’s tough to have, for example, accommodation paid for in Venice tonight that they can’t use ... It’s really a situation nothing anybody can do to change.” Matilda, Penni and Angus Milton before embarking on the flight to Doha. Their connecting flight to Venice turned around. Credit: Penni Milton

Penni and the kids have spent two nights sleeping on the floor of Doha airport. There are queues for food and spots near power points. “Hopefully, a hotel, a shower and a sleep in a bed, but I’m not counting those chickens until they’re hatched,” Penni said of what’s next. “We can’t do anything, we can’t go anywhere until the airspace opens.” Impacts and warnings for Australian travellers For travellers, Iran’s willingness to strike civilian infrastructure in response to the US-Israel bombings adds new concern.



“The fact that Iran fired missiles against hotels and the airport in Dubai kind of raises the stakes,” said Justin Wastnage from Griffith University’s Institute for Tourism. “The full impact depends on how long the conflict takes,” he said.



On Saturday, nearly 1000 flights of approximately 4200 flights scheduled to arrive in Middle Eastern countries were cancelled, data from Cirium shows. Adding in scheduled departures, the total number of cancellations exceeded 1800 flights in a day.



“Some flights already en route today were diverted,” said Juliette Alpha. “Most of those were from Middle Eastern carriers. A few non-Middle Eastern airlines had already departed at the time of the military action today.” Emirates cancelled 108 of 269 scheduled flights. Etihad cancelled 80 of 191 scheduled flights. Qatar cancelled 174 of 304 flights.



In Australia, Virgin and Qatar have cancelled flights and urged customers to consult the Australian government for travel advice. Four Virgin Australia flights operated by Qatar Airways made midair U-turns back to Australia on Saturday evening due to the closure of Qatari airspace.

title: Rebuilding, regrouping, unreliable or overripe: The AFL’s 17-year Victorian era is fading
text:
Richmond’s rebuild of their list is among the more uncompromising yet undertaken. It will be years before they compete for the flag again, a scenario their fans accept. Carlton, by losing Charlie Curnow and Tom De Koning, have accepted a withdrawal from contention mode to refresh with elite kids (father-sons and Jagga Smith). The Blues have assessed their position astutely. They can aspire to the top 10, not to the top six. New Blues: Carlton are in a regroup phase after losing some big names in the off-season. Credit: AFL Photos via Getty Images Essendon’s road has been bumpy under Brad Scott, who is attempting a cultural rebuild in the face of Zach Merrett’s failed defection. It is a slow process for the Baby-step Bombers. Even if they surprisingly rose to a wildcard final, they’re not upending the best. Melbourne have put the post 2021 premiership Demons to bed, as Max Gawn acknowledged. Petracca and Oliver’s exits, and new coach Steven King’s pressing of the reset button means they are in a position much like Carlton, ie, regrouping and not contenders.

The Bulldogs are harder to read than James Joyce’s Ulysses. They smash the weak. They haven’t beaten the strong, despite Marcus Bontempelli and Tom Liberatore’s heroics, Ed Richards’ rise and Sam Darcy’s freakish gifts. Until they defend successfully, they won’t see late September. But the Dogs at least have the raw material to challenge. Ready to challenge: Marcus Bontempelli’s Western Bulldogs. Credit: AFL Photos via Getty Images Hawthorn, fresh from consecutive finals series under Sam Mitchell, seemed ripe to press for their 14th flag. Will Day’s continued foot and shoulder ailments, alas, is a major impediment to a preliminary or grand final berth. Had they somehow landed Merrett – harder than landing on Mars, in retrospect – they would have coverage in the middle, where Jai Newcombe is the sole A grader, sans Day. By dint of their risqué recruiting, when Tom De Koning, Sam Flanders, Liam Ryan and Jack Silvagni landed for near enough to $4 million, the Saints have, unexpectedly, become the most compelling storyline of the Victorian contingent. They have quietly accumulated young talent, and should make the second week of the wildcard system (ie, top eight).

In the 60th anniversary of that solitary premiership, it would be asking too much for the Saints to take the grail. They’re not ready. North Melbourne’s progress has been so laboured that the natives – and some of the 1990s crew – are losing patience. This year, the story is about Alastair Clarkson, as he runs out of runway. Of the Victorian 10, that leaves the team that has defied the draft’s gravity for so long that we take for granted they’ll hang around: Geelong. Cats captain Patrick Dangerfield with Bailey Smith. Credit: AFL Photos via Getty Images The Cats can remain thereabouts, having steadily infused an ageing list with younger players. The difficulty for Chris Scott is that they can’t extract more from Patrick Dangerfield, Jeremy Cameron or Tom Stewart. Bailey Smith’s career season might not be repeated. The growth will need to come from others.

Compare the Victorian prospectus to what we once called, with unconscious bias, as “the interstate clubs”. Loading Brisbane’s abundance of talent, maturity and scope for improvement – aided by recruits Oscar Allen, Sam Draper and Daniel Annable – puts the Lions in the best spot on the grid. Will Lachie Neale’s personal issues drag them down? Unlikely. They won the grand final and navigated the first half without him. Behind the Lions are two other northern state clubs, Sydney and Damien Hardwick’s Suns. Sydney are poised for a rebound in Dean Cox’s second year, having returned to the proven playbook of purloining a key forward from the Melbourne fishbowl. Charlie Curnow will provide the avenue the Swans have not had since Lance Franklin’s final season. Tom Papley and Callum Mills were injured for much of 2025, and will be de facto recruits, too.

The Gold Coast midfield of Matt Rowell, Noah Anderson, Touk Miller and the luxury item of Petracca shapes as the competition’s most formidable when Rowell is back after the early rounds. Hardwick has serious talent at his disposal, much of it generated by the academy. An all-Queensland grand final, a fantasy two years ago, is well within the bounds of possibilities. Loading Fremantle have set the table for success, too. They have a core of players moving into their prime, headed by Victorian trio Caleb Serong, Hayden Young and Andrew Brayshaw. They should be inside the top six in ’26. Adelaide’s minor premiership was unwound by dismal finals performances. Yet, they, too, should be a finals team. Midfield quality, outside of Jordan Dawson and (sometimes) Izak Rankine, was their undoing last September. GWS have a list with enough quality to compete for the flag. Their pre-season, alas, has included a worrisome toll of injury, headed by Tom Green’s season-ending knee and Sam Taylor’s bothersome hamstring.

title: The NRL has picked a bad time for global domination
text:
The NRL hasn’t been shy about their plans to globalise the code. They’re like the property owners with the best house on the street – complete with great renos and good extension plans – but deep down they just want to move streets.

They’ll struggle because their direct rival, the 15-man code, is already established and could deliver an international TV audience of 20 million per round of the new seven-round Nations Championships, which starts in July and concludes in November.

France v Italy in the Six Nations peaked at 8.8m in France alone, England v Wales hit 5m in England, and England v Ireland peaked at 1m in the Emerald Isle. Add South Africa, Australia, New Zealand, Fiji and Japan to the Nations Championship in July and that 20m figure becomes attainable - it might even be conservative.

The Nations Championship is the match to light the petrol
